noeuds.titre: Constance Garnett
Revised by Saklovskaya & Rice:
DAVID McDUFF:
Richard Pevear and
Larissa Volokhonsky:
Constance Garnett:
CHAPTART
CHAPTER I
On an exceptionally hot evening early in July a young man came out of the garret in which h
dged in S. Place and walked slowly, as though in hesitation, towards K. bridg
He had successfully avoided meeting his landlady on the staircase. His garret was under the roof
of a high, five-storied house and was more like a cupboard than a room. The landlady who provided
nim witn garret, anners, and attendance, lvea on une noor below, and every ume ne went out ne
was obliged to nass her kitchen. the door of which invariablv stood onen. And each time he nassed
the young man had a sick, frightened feeling, which made him scowl and feel ashamed. He was
hopelessly in debt to his landlady, and was afraid of meeting her.
This was not because he was cowardly and abject, quite the contrary; but for some time past he
had been in an overstrained irritable condition, verging on hypochondria. He had become so
completely absorbed in himself, and isolated from his fellows that he dreaded meeting, not only his
landlady, bul anyone al all. He was crusned by poverty, bul te anxietes or nis posion nad ol lale
ceased to weigh upon him. He had given up attending to matters of practical importance: he had losi
all desire to do so. Nothing that any landlady could do had a real terror for him. But to be stopped
on the stairs, to be forced to listen to her trivial, irrelevant gossip, to pestering demands for payment,
threats and complaints, and to rack his brains for excuses, to prevaricate, to lie-no, rather than that,
ne would creep down the stalrs nke a cal and sup out unseen
This evening, however, on coming out into the street, he became acutely aware of his fears.
"I want to attempt a thing like that and am frightened by these trifles" he thought. with an odd
smile. "Hm... yes, all is in a man's hands and he lets it all slip from cowardice, that's an axiom. It
vould be interesting to know what it is men are most afraid of. Taking a new step, uttering a new
vord is what they fear most... But I am talking too much. It's because I chatter that I do nothing. O
pernaps it is tat 1 cnatter decause i ao noting. I ve learnea to natter inis last monu, lying or days
together in mv den thinking of lack the Giant-killer. Whv am i coins there now Am i canable of
that? Is that serious? It is not serious at all. It's simply a fantasy to amuse myself; a plaything! Yes,
maybe it is a plaything."
he heat in the street was terrible. and the airlessness. the bustle and the naster scattolding. bricks
and dust all about him, and that special Petersburg stench, so familiar to all who are unable to get
but of town in summer—all worked painfully upon the young man's already overwrought ner
The insulterable stench from the pot houses, which are particulaly numerous in that part of ves.
town, and the drunken men whom he met continually, although it was a working dav. completed the
revolting miserv of the picture. An expression of the profoundest disoust gleamed for a moment in
the young man's refined face. He was, by the way, exceptionally handsome, above the average in
height, slim, well-built, with beautiful dark eyes and dark brown hair. Soon he sank into deep thought
or more accurately speaking into a complete blankness of mind; he walked along not observing what
was about him and not caring to observe it. From time to time. he would mutter something, from
the hahit of talkino to himselt to which he had ist confessed At these moments he would become
conscious that his ideas were sometimes in a tangle and that he was very weak; for two days he had
scarcely tasted food.
